I am an introvert and I love it. And I'm not alone. Introverts are everywhere, and our quiet approach to life, our need for solitary time, isn't a flaw - it's a gift. But as an introvert it's not always easy to realise how wonderful you are. The world feels like a place that rewards extroverts. Where being loud is mistaken for being confident and happy. Where everyone has something to say, but nobody listens. A world of open-plan offices, networking parties, and big personalities. For those who speak softly, it's easy to feel left out. As a child, I blended into the background. Many thought that I had little to say or that I simply didn't like others. But that wasn't true. People often think that introverts are shy, or antisocial. But these are misconceptions. Introverts, like anyone, can find socialising fun. But while parties leave extroverts energised, after some time, introverts need to recharge... away from everyone. There is a scientific theory for this. There are two important chemicals found in all our brains - dopamine and acetylcholine. Dopamine is like a hit of energy when we take risks or meet new people - and it makes extroverts feel great. But introverts are more sensitive to dopamine and get quickly over-stimulated. That's why we prefer the more slow-burn feeling we get when our brains release acetylcholine. That happens when we concentrate, read, or focus our minds. It makes us introverts feel relaxed, alert and content. But it barely registers with extroverts. Of course, like anything, it's a sliding scale. You can lean one way or another. Or be a bit of both - known as an ambivert. Now I understand myself better, I am deeply grateful for how I am. Instead of filling up space with small talk, I listen patiently and make my words matter. I have few friends but our connection is deep. I love spending time alone. It's where the chaos of a long day can finally settle. I can reflect and listen to my thoughts, and eventually reconnect with myself. Only after that am I ready to share with the world again. I have learnt strategies for finding comfort in our noisy world - from using music to create bubbles of peace, to escaping to a quiet park at lunchtime. I adore the intensity and chaotic beauty of the world. But it's in quiet spaces where I feel truly at home. If introversion were more valued by society, it could make a massive difference to our collective future. The unique attributes of introverts really are a deep, quiet strength. And as Gandhi, put it, "In a gentle way, you can shake the world." Thanks for watching. Don't forget to subscribe and click the bell to receive notifications for new videos. See you again soon!

The part of your brain that's essential for forming all of your consciously held memories is in your hippocampus. Where neurogenesis, which is the birth of new neurons, happens. The last step is retrieval. So you got the information in, to remember it, you want to get the information out. So this means you can now activate this neural circuit. And so you can recall, revisit, reminisce, know something that happened or that you learned before. We can actually see what's going on through brain scans through functional MRI imaging. We should talk for a bit  about how world wars happen,  one because the world looks like this right now,  two because it is on the  verge of looking like this, three because, in things that did  not get discussed in the news due   to Iran and Israel getting all of the attention, this happened— welcome by the way to the party, the Philippines, four because North Korea just has  impeccable timing with these things as well, five because some earlier dominoes falling  in Russia and then Syria are how we got to   the current aerial war between Israel and Iran, and six because there is an agenda  item on next week’s NATO heads of   state summit that might help mitigate the problem. Now, if you will permit me a moment to catch my  breath as we look at an extremely Dutch photo let’s talk about World War III. What defines a world war seems  like a good starting point. It is not just that a whole  bunch of pairs of counties   are all engaged in various wars at the same time. It is that they are all somehow connected to  one another, and what happens on one front will   effect all of the others, either directly  or indirectly through a third conflict. In fact, it is that interconnectivity  that creates the risk of a broader   conflict starting for no reason other  than other conflicts are happening. Something like Russia invaded Ukraine, which caused Israel to begin bombing Iran,  which then entangles the  United States in the process,  which triggers a mainland invasion of Taiwan, which prompts North Korea to attack South Korea,  as well as a subcontinental  showdown between India and Pakistan. It is basically a series of dominoes falling over,   except instead of having the mild inconvenience  of having to pick them all up afterward, you have tens of millions of dead bodies to bury—  or maybe they just be vaporized,  thereby sparing us the trouble. The joys of nuclear annihilation aside,   we should spend a moment discussing  how exactly world wars come about. Not too long ago, we examined this problem  in the context of the Trump Doctrine. The key phrase is “wars of opportunism,”  and the idea is straightforward if you  understand some simple lines on maps logic. Let’s review it from the perspective of  Taiwan, since that war has not started. Yet. Under normal circumstances, we have the two  parties in the midst of a contentious peace.   Neither one is exactly happy about  the way the world works for them. But trying to militarily change  that will only make the problem   worse because of the costs and risks involved. That is, they both understand roughly what  the outcome of a war between them would be,  in terms of the likelihood that mainland wins  versus Taipei maintaining  control of its own sovereignty. They also recognize that a whole  bunch of costs will result from a war,   which perhaps they internalize in terms  of sovereignty in the manner seen here. Thus, rather than fight, they  preserve a weird division of   Taiwanese sovereignty that is roughly in line with that war expectation. It’s better for everyone that way. The problem is that the world around them  is a busy one, filled with other conflicts. And a war between the PRC and Taiwan is  not a war between the PRC and Taiwan— it is a war between the PRC  and Taiwan and the United   States and Japan and South Korea and perhaps more. Heck, maybe Russia will help out on the  other side. Beijing could only be so lucky. The thing is, though, that help  presupposes that it is available.   The United States would like to help  Taiwan, sure, but maybe it is busy   with Iran at the moment—emphasis  on the “at the moment” qualifier. Indeed, the momentary weakness creates a  window of opportunity for the mainland. With the United States sidetracked, the  PRC’s ability to conquer Taiwan will   significantly increase—but, critically,  only while that distraction continues. The concern for fans of global  stability is that what the PRC   expects to win during that window of opportunity, even after accounting for its costs of war, is better than the greatest degree  of sovereignty that Taiwan could   begrudgingly concede to the mainland, once  the United States is no longer distracted. See, these demands are not compatible. And that is a war of opportunism. Moreover, this is not a hypothetical  discussion of the current global   crisis that we are witnessing. Let me  take you back in time for a moment. Because Russia was first distracted and   then exhausted by a much longer  war than expected in Ukraine, Azerbaijan took the opportunity to renew the  Ngorno-Karabakh conflict in September 2023. Similarly, the reason that the decisive  offensive in the Syrian Civil War happened   in December 2024 was again because  Russia could not muster a response. Heck, we can trace the current aerial war between   Iran and Israel back to that.  While chaos reigned in Syria, Israel took the opportunity to  take out Syria’s air defenses. Hmm,   that also sounds like a strike of opportunism. Today, Israeli planes are taking  a route across the helpless Syria,  through the friendly skies of Kurdish Iraq, and over to Iran to drop their payloads. Assad’s fall solved one of the one of the  biggest problems that had vexed hawkish Israelis,   who wanted to attack Iran’s program for decades:  how the heck to actually get over there safely. And it is also something that might  not last forever, depending on the   direction of the Syrian government and  how fast it may reacquire air defenses. You know, if we really wanted to  dive deep into this, another reason   that Israel is doing this now is because  Israel had absolutely battered Hezbollah after Hezbollah thought it was a good  idea to fight following Hamas’s attack, which itself happened largely because Hamas was   worried that Israel and Saudi  Arabia would align together. The only reason that we have not gone into a full   world war is because there has not  been enough strain on the system. Yet. But things like an American  intervention into Iran,  coupled with a hefty response  against U.S. bases in the region,  or the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, or an unconventional attack on the U.S. homeland, or an internal rebellion putting  pressure on the Ayatollah,  adding some lions on maps, and encouraging the United States to keep going … all of those things could be just  enough to push the world over the edge. That is how you get the PRC invading Taiwan, the United States trying to tie that down,  a clash in Korea, India taking the opportunity to revisit some   disputed regions that it has  with its communist neighbor, Pakistan deciding that then would  be a good time to capture Kashmir, Saudi Arabia doing who knows what with Iran and maybe Yemeni Houthis as well. And after that, your guess for  the next war is as good as mine.  Let the dominoes fall where they may be. The situation is not hopeless, however.  There is something that can be done   to solve the problem. A solution  to the opportunism, if you will. It will be expensive,  but then again the alternative  may be even more expensive. You have likely heard about the  new defense spending target of  5% of GDP that will be the centerpiece  of next week’s NATO summit. Well, that is exactly the type of thing  that counteracts the opportunism incentive. Back during the Cold War, you may recall  that the United States operated under a  “two-war construct”—the idea  that it should always have  enough military force to handle two conflicts simultaneously. It cost the United States a whole lot of  money, but it served a purpose: it prevented   the start of one war from triggering  another war through the domino effect. Well, then the Cold War came to  an end, and U.S. military power   has slowly eroded to the point where  it definitely is a one-war construct. If we jump ahead to NATO’s 2014 Wales Summit,   you started to get a push back. It  was site of the declaration that 2% of GDP should be spent on  defense, which is today’s status quo. Part of the goal there was to give NATO as  a whole a two-war construct, specifically   designed so that increased European defense  spending could handle a conflict with Russia, while the United States would handle security  affairs in the Indo Pacific. Remember,   this was the era of the attempted Obama pivot. The problem for today is that the  scale of the potential conflicts   has increased. 2% of GDP may not  be enough to cover the situation. Trump’s foreign policy frames itself as a “prioritization imperative,” where this time  the United States is really pivoting to Asia,   and the United States really means it. But that does not change how the fates of Europe  and the United States are still intertwined. Nor does it change how there are still  mutual interests in the Middle East that   Europe does not have the capacity to  cover but that the United States does. In fact, you might remember that this  disconnect came up in the leaked Signal chat. Now, what might be able to handle  that is spending an additional  1.5% of GDP by the entire alliance, including  an increase in U.S. defense spending.  3.5% total is a lot of money to throw around. And bear in mind here that  the gain is not just from the   direct additional coercive power  that having more arms gives you. It is also the deterrent power of  stopping countries from initiating   a war of opportunism, and imposing all of  the corresponding costs of conflict on you. Perhaps equally important is what accounts for the   remaining 1.5% that takes states  all the way up to the full 5%. That is supposed to include  infrastructure spending,   which immediately conjures up thoughts of  bridges that would help deploy the hardware. But might it include a broader defense  industrial base as well? Because wars   of opportunism are not just about an initial  distraction. They are also about attrition. If a major war persists for years,  shell hunger may eventually empty   your warehouses, at which point you  are a golden target for opportunism. And to further the problem, wars of opportunism  themselves tend to last a long time. You will recall how most wars end in  some type of negotiated settlement. If,   for example, there is a disagreement  over the eventual outcome of a conflict, the war itself reveals that  information and thus resolves   the bargaining friction that caused  the war to start in the first place. Well, a war of opportunism begins because  of a moment of vulnerability. Fighting does   not reveal meaningful information in this case. The parties are at war because they know  the consequences of what will happen,   and one of them finds the alternative even worse. So increasing defense spending is not  just about stopping any old war. It is   about stopping the wars that are more  likely to be especially destructive. Might 5% be the answer? Again, that is a question for  the heads of state at The Hague.  And it is worth noting that  there is no guarantee there. But the problem is not about to go away. Even  if the United States does not end up fighting an   elongated a war with Iran, the underlying security  environment is not looking good right now. Hey, Hollywood! If you’re watching this, I have an idea for you! You’re all about making disaster movies, right? Especially ones where astronomical events cause The End of the World As We Know It? Like, when the ocean currents got messed up and New York City froze? Or when the planets lined up, and Earth ripped open? Well, here’s my pitch: What if the Earth just stopped spinning? It may sound implausible -- even to you, Hollywood -- but physicists /have/ actually considered what we could expect if our planet’s rotation were to stop. And I gotta say: It’s not pretty. And it may actually happen! But just … over the course of a couple billion years. First, let’s imagine that the Earth’s rotation just stopped suddenly -- like, because of an impact with some giant, planet-sized object. If that were to happen, and you somehow survived it, the first effects would be pretty obvious. Because, everything would be flying away at supersonic speeds. That’s because Earth rotates at about 1700 km/h at the equator. And if the planet suddenly stopped, its atmosphere would keep going, creating a windstorm way stronger than anything we’ve ever experienced. Anything that wasn’t anchored to bedrock would be swept away … … cities, forests, rock, and topsoil -- all just zooming sideways and crashing into each other. Meanwhile, the oceans would lurch into huge tsunamis. Just think how cool the special effects would be! But the truth is, if Earth really were to stop spinning, it wouldn’t happen all at once. It would do it very slowly. And in fact, that process is already underway -- mostly because of the moon’s gravitation pulling on us, a phenomenon known as tidal braking. As a result, the length of a day on Earth is getting longer, by about 14 thousandths of a second, every 100 years. So theoretically, after several billion years, our rotational spin would decay enough that the Earth would just … stop. And OK, yes: By this time, the sun might have already swelled up to a red giant and devoured everything. But, for the sake of argument: What would happen as the Earth’s rotation slowed way down? Well, most of the effects would come from a big shift in our planet’s equatorial bulge. See, the spin of the Earth creates a bulge around the equator -- an accumulation of mass that makes a cute little spare tire around the planet’s waist. If the spin slowed down, the first thing that would stop bulging would be Earth’s most fluid component: the oceans. Computer models predict that the oceans’ mass would retreat from the equator and move toward the poles -- where Earth’s gravity would be the strongest. This redistribution of water would completely submerge northern Europe, Russia, and parts of South America, while my home here in Montana would now be oceanfront property. And, if that weren’t enough, there would also be changes that would happen on a more day-to-day basis. By which I mean, the whole idea of “a day” would be totally different. After all, Earth’s rotation controls how much daylight different places get around the world. If our rotation slowed down, parts of Earth would get much more daylight, and much longer nights. By the time our planet came to a dead stop, a “day” would be entirely controlled by our orbit around the sun: Daytime would be six months long, followed by a six month night. But there’s one more major thing that would change -- something less tangible than where the oceans are, or the length of our day. But in terms of our planet’s habitability, it’s probably the most important thing. Without its rotation, the Earth would no longer have a magnetic field. We’re one of the few rocky planets in the solar system that’s lucky enough to have a nice, robust magnetic field, or magnetosphere, around us. This magnetosphere protects us from cosmic radiation, and a lot of the most dangerous radiation from the sun. And we owe its existence, in part, to our planet’s outer core of metallic rock. Molten metal inside the Earth rises and cools and then sinks again, creating convection currents, while also spinning like a dynamo, to create electrical and magnetic energy. If the Earth stopped spinning, this magnetic field would gradually fade, exposing us to more and more cosmic radiation until life became -- scarce, if not impossible. As a cautionary example -- you know who used to have a magnetic field, and then lost it? Mars. Astronomers think that, if Mars was ever habitable, it was four billion years ago, when its rocks show that it had a strong magnetosphere. When the magnetic field disappeared, so did its atmosphere and, as far as we can tell, its ability to sustain life. So, there’s a disaster scenario for you, Hollywood -- one that’s totally scientifically plausible but also, in its own way, butt-clenchingly terrifying. You’re welcome!
